utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay , so our next patient is christopher watson . date of birth 04/12/1934 . mr . watson is- is an 86-year-old male who returns in follow-up for adult hydrocephalus . this is his first visit since undergoing sh- shunt surgery , excuse me , on august 1st , 2020 .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: i have followed mr . watson since may of 2020 when he first presented with eight to ten years of progressive gait impairment , cognitive impairment , and decreased bladder control . we established a diagnosis of adult hydrocephalus with a spino catheter protocol in june 2020 , and he underwent shunt surgery on august 1st , 2020 . a medtronic strata programmable shunt in the ventricular peritoneal configuration was programmed at a level 2.0 was placed .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: good morning , mr . watson , who is the nice lady you have with you today ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: hi , doc . this is my daughter , theresa . she is the one who helps me keep things straight .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: mr . watson , have you been sick or been hosp -- hospitalized since your shu- shunt surgery three months ago .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: uh , not that i recall .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no , he has n't . he has been doing much better .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: good , i'm glad to hear that .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: before surgery , you were having some progressive problems that affected your walking , thinking , and bladder control . are these symptoms better , worse , or unchanged ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: his walking has improved . he's now able to walk to the dining room just fine . but he could not do that before surgery . he still has times where i have to help him .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: that's impressive , mr . watson .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah , i guess .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: now his bladder control did improve some after surgery , but now seems to have leveled out . so he is wearing diapers .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: no issues with sores from wearing the diaper ? and any decreased or painful urination ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no , not at all .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: and how about cognition ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i think i'm better . i do n't feel lost when talking to someone anymore .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: dad and i both think my sister , hannah , summarized it best . she said " i feel like i can have a normal conversation with him again , "

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: that's so wonderful ! now have you had any headaches or pain where the shunt is ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no . has n't bothered me one bit .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . and any pain in your belly at all ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: ca n't say that i have .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay , sounds like you have been improving . alrighty , mr . watson , i'm going to step out of the room while my resident comes in to do a few tests for me . is that all right ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: sounds good .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay , i'm going to go out and review the ct scan you had last month , and then we'll chat about it when i come in .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: all righty , mr . watson , so you definitely have made some improvements since the shunt surgery . now on ct scan though i do believe i see a tiny bit of blood fluid collection in the right parietal region . now , i do n't believe that that's causing any symptoms , and i , it actually was n't documented by the radiologist when he read the ct scan .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: so what does that mean ?

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: just that i would like to leave the shunt set for another two to three months before we go in changing it . like i said , if you're not having any headaches or pain and you have improved so that's all good for me . so really there's , there's nothing to worry about .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: all right so i'll come back in three months ?

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yep , that's right .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i do have a question for you though .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah , of course .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: do you think i will be able to drive again ?

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: ah man , mr . watson , i knew you were gon na ask me that one . i think at this point it's unlikely that your movement speed will improve to the level that would be needed for you to pass the driver safety evaluation . now , i will say that i do occasionally have patients who surprise me by improving over a nine to 12 month range . and that , that definitely could be you . you could definitely pass it at that point . so if you improve and we can continue to talk about that then , ya know , i think it could be possible , um , and i'd be happy to recommend it .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: now , you could definitely also enroll in a driver safety program without my recommendation . so you could kind of do that preemptively , and then we could continue to watch for your improvement as we go .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay . i kinda figured that anyway but i just thought i'd ask . i do n't like to burden anyone when i just need to run to the store but i also know that i need to be safe .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah , your safety is our number one priority and , ya know , i'm sure your daughters do n't mind as much , right , they want to keep you here and have you safe . but , i overall am very pleased with how much you have improved . and so , i'm sure when we talk again in three months , we'll continue to be on that upward trajectory or improvement .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i sure hope so , doc .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: we really appreciate all that you've done . do we make the appointment out front ?

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah , that would be great . the girls up front will take care of you and get you settled for three months from now , and you both take care , all right ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: thanks

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: thank you .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: all righty , so ros is constitutional significant for impaired ability to carry out daily functions . negative for fever or unintentional weight loss . gu is significant for urinary incontinence and wearing a diaper . negative for genital sores , decreased or painful output . neurological significant for continued walking impairment and cognitive impairment . negative for headaches , recent falls , or hallucinations . psychiatric is negative for depression , excessive worrying , or mood swings .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: let's go ahead and use the short pe . vitals , let's see , blood pressure is 124/80 , heart rate 64 , respirations 18 , weight 174 pounds , and pain zero out of 10 .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: mr . watson is a pleasant and cooperative man who's able to converse easily through , um , though his daughter does add some details . the shunt sight was clean , dry , and intact with a confirmed setting of two . um he was tested um for recent and remote memory , attention span , and concentration in fund of knowledge . he scored a 26 out of 30 on the mmse when tested with spelling and 25 out of 30 when tested with calculations . of note , he was able to get two of the three memory words with cuing , and the third with multiple choice . this was a slight improvement over his initial score of 23 out of 30 with calculations and 24 out of 30 with spelling . and at the time he was unable um in the previous test , excuse me , he was unable to remember any memory words with cuing without and only one with multiple choice .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: gait testing using the tinetti assessment tool . he was tested without an assistive device and received a gait score of six to eight out of 12 and a balance score of 12 out of 16 for a total score of 18 to 20 out of 28 . this is slightly improved from his initial score of 15 to 17 out of 28 .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: cranial nerves , pupils are equal . eoms are intact . face symmetric . no disarchria . motor normal for bulk and strength . coordination slow from no- finger to nose . of note , the ct scan from the head of 10/15/2020 shows a frontal horn span at the level of foramen of monro of 4.6 centimeters with a third ventricular contour that is flat with a span of 10 millimeters . by my reading there is a tiny amount of blood in the right front region with a tiny subdermal collection . this was not noticed or noted by the radiologist who stated any extra axial fluid collections . there is also substantial small vessel ischemic change .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: diagnosis adult hydrocephalus , gait impairment , urinary incontinence and urgency , and cognitive impairment .

detail_list.summary: Mr. Watson is an 86-year-old male presenting for follow-up of adult hydrocephalus three months post-shunt surgery.
Adult hydrocephalus follow-up.
The patient has a history of progressive gait impairment, cognitive impairment, and decreased bladder control, treated with a Medtronic Strata programmable shunt set at 2.0.
The doctor asks if the patient has been hospitalized since his shunt surgery.
The patient reports no hospitalizations since surgery.
The doctor asks about the status of the patient's walking, thinking, and bladder control symptoms.
The patient's walking has improved, allowing him to walk to the dining room, though he still requires some assistance.
Bladder control improved initially but has leveled off, and the patient uses diapers.
The doctor asks about diaper-related skin issues and urinary symptoms.
The patient denies skin sores or painful urination.
The doctor asks about the patient's cognitive status.
The patient reports improved cognition and feels less lost during conversations.
The doctor asks about headaches or pain at the shunt site.
The patient denies headaches or shunt site pain.
The doctor asks about abdominal pain.
The patient denies abdominal pain.
CT scan shows a tiny fluid collection in the right parietal region, which the doctor deems asymptomatic.
The patient asks for the clinical significance of the CT finding.
The doctor advises maintaining the current shunt setting for two to three months.
The patient asks if he should return in three months.
The doctor confirms a follow-up appointment in three months.
The patient asks if he will be able to drive again.
The doctor states it is unlikely the patient can pass a driver's safety evaluation now, but improvement over 9-12 months might make it possible.
The doctor suggests the patient can enroll in a driver safety program independently.

Review of systems: constitutional (impaired daily function, no fever/weight loss), GU (incontinence, diaper use, no sores/pain), neuro (walking/cognitive impairment, no headaches/falls/hallucinations), psych (no depression/mood swings).
Vitals: BP 124/80, HR 64, RR 18, weight 174 lbs, pain 0/10.
Physical exam: shunt site clean/intact at 2.0; MMSE 26/30 (spelling) and 25/30 (calculations), showing improvement.
Gait exam: Tinetti score 18-20/28, showing slight improvement.
Cranial nerves intact, motor normal, coordination slow; CT scan shows frontal horn span 4.6 cm, tiny right frontal blood/subdural collection, and small vessel ischemic change.
Assessment: adult hydrocephalus, gait impairment, urinary incontinence/urgency, and cognitive impairment.
